items.title: Obesity and endometrial carcinogenesis
items.contentHtml:
Main risk factor: obesity

  • Obesity is the main risk factor for peripheral conversion of androgens to estrogens in adipose tissue, mediated by the enzyme aromatase, generating chronic hyperestrogenism without progestational opposition.
  • The relative risk increases 3-5 times in women with body mass index >30 kg/m²; Each increase of 5 units in body mass index increases the risk by 50-60%.
  • Prolonged estrogen exposure stimulates the proliferation of the endometrium, favoring atypical hyperplasia and subsequent endometrioid adenocarcinoma (Type I).
  • In clinical practice, weight loss reduces the incidence and improves the oncological prognosis.

items.title: Postmenopausal bleeding as a sentinel symptom
items.contentHtml:
Abnormal postmenopausal uterine bleeding

  • abnormal postmenopausal uterine bleeding (defined as any vaginal bleeding after 12 months of amenorrhea) is the presenting symptom in >90% of cases and requires ruling out endometrial cancer.
  • Endometrial thickness by transvaginal ultrasound <5 mm has a value negative predictive >99% for cancer; if ≥5 mm, endometrial biopsy is indicated.
  • Differential diagnosis: endometrial atrophy, polyps, endometrial hyperplasia, hormonal therapy, atrophic cervicitis.
  • The presence of bleeding in a postmenopausal woman without hormonal therapy is an oncological emergency until proven otherwise.

items.foco: Endometrial biopsy is the diagnostic gold standard
items.title: Gold standard diagnosis
items.contentHtml:
Endometrial biopsy is the diagnostic gold standard

  • Endometrial biopsy is the diagnostic gold standard, with a sensitivity of 90-98% for cancer; It can be performed with a Pipelle cannula in the office or by hysteroscopy with directed curettage.
  • A negative endometrial biopsy does not exclude cancer if clinical suspicion is high; Fractionated uterine curettage under anesthesia or diagnostic hysteroscopy is recommended.
  • The histopathological study reports histological type, tumor grade (G1-G3) and presence of stromal invasion.
  • Cervical cytology (Papan smear) is not suitable for primary diagnosis, but may suggest cervical involvement.

items.title: Type I: endometrioid
items.contentHtml:
Type I: endometrioid, estrogen-dependent

  • Type I (endometrioid) represents 80-85% of endometrial cancers, it is estrogen-dependent, low grade (G1-G2) and arises on atypical endometrial hyperplasia.
  • Risk factors: obesity, polycystic ovary syndrome, nulliparity, exposure to estrogens unopposed exogenous, tamoxifen.
  • It has a good prognosis (5-year survival >85%) and usually occurs in perimenopausal or early postmenopausal women.
  • Associated with mutations in PTEN, PIK3CA, KRAS and unstable microsatellites; the expression of estrogen and progesterone receptors is frequently positive.

items.foco: Type II: serous, non-estrogen-dependent, worse prognosis
items.title: Type II: serous
items.contentHtml:
Type II: serous, non-estrogen-dependent, worse prognosis

  • The Type II (serous, clear cell, carcinosarcoma) is non-estrogen-dependent, high grade, arises on atrophic endometrium and represents 10-20% of cases but causes >50% of deaths.
  • Risk factors: advanced age, endometrial atrophy, African American race, history of pelvic radiation.
  • It is associated with mutations in TP53 and chromosomal instability; It frequently presents lymphovascular invasion and early peritoneal dissemination.
  • Treatment requires complete surgical staging with omentectomy and pelvic and para-aortic lymphadenectomy; adverse prognosis with 5-year survival <50%.

items.foco: Surgical staging with hysterectomy and lymphadenectomy
items.title: Surgical staging
items.contentHtml:
Surgical staging with hysterectomy and lymphadenectomy

  • Surgical staging includes total extrafascial hysterectomy, bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy, pelvic and para-aortic lymphadenectomy, and peritoneal cytology.
  • It is performed by laparotomy or laparoscopy; Lymphadenectomy allows determining lymph node involvement, key for stage IIIC.
  • Surgical staging is the standard for all histological types except stage IA G1 with fertile desires.
  • The stage is defined according to the International Federation of Gynecology and Obstetrics (FIGO) 2009 classification; The surgical stage replaced the clinical stage since 1988.

items.title: PTEN mutation
items.contentHtml:
Genetic marker: mutation in PTEN

  • The mutation in PTEN (tumor suppressor gene on chromosome 10q23) is the most frequent alteration in Type I endometrial cancer, present in 40-80% of cases.
  • PTEN regulates the PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway; its loss of function promotes cell proliferation and resistance to apoptosis.
  • It is also observed in atypical endometrial hyperplasia, suggesting an early event in endometrioid carcinogenesis.
  • Immunohistochemistry for PTEN can be used in differential diagnosis of endometrial lesions, but is not routine in standard clinical practice.

items.foco: Women with Lynch syndrome are at high risk
items.title: Lynch syndrome and endometrial risk
items.contentHtml:
Women with Lynch syndrome are at high risk

  • Women with Lynch syndrome (hereditary non-polyposis colorectal cancer) have a cumulative risk of endometrial cancer of 40-60%, similar to or greater than that of colorectal cancer.
  • It is due to germline mutations in mating repair genes (MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2); the average age of presentation is 48-50 years.
  • Annual screening with transvaginal ultrasound and endometrial biopsy is recommended starting at 30-35 years, or prophylactic hysterectomy once parity is complete.
  • Immunohistochemistry for mating repair proteins in the tumor or the microsatellite instability test helps identify candidates for genetic study.

items.foco: Standard treatment: total hysterectomy with bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy
items.title: Standard surgical treatment
items.contentHtml:
Standard treatment: total hysterectomy with bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy

  • The standard treatment for early-stage endometrial cancer is total hysterectomy with bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy and surgical staging.
  • In stage IA G1 with desire for fertility, hormonal therapy with progestogens (medroxyprogesterone acetate 200-600 mg/day or megestrol 160 mg/day) and strict follow-up.
  • For advanced stages (III-IV), adjuvant radiotherapy (vaginal brachytherapy or external radiotherapy) and/or chemotherapy with platinum and taxanes is added.
  • Para-aortic lymphadenectomy is indicated in high-risk tumors (G3, Type II, myometrial invasion >50%).

items.foco: Use of progestogens in early disease and fertile desire
items.title: Progestogens in fertile preservation
items.contentHtml:
Use of progestogens in early disease and fertile desire

  • In women with stage IA G1 endometrial cancer (endometrioid) who wish to preserve fertility, conservative treatment with oral progestogens (megestrol acetate 160 mg/day or medroxyprogesterone acetate 200-600 mg/day) or intrauterine-releasing device is indicated of levonorgestrel.
  • The complete response rate is 70-80%, with a median time of 6 months; Control endometrial biopsy is required every 3-6 months.
  • Contraindications: non-endometrioid histological type (serous, clear cell), grade G2-G3, myometrial invasion >50% or extrauterine disease.
  • After parity is completed, total hysterectomy with definitive bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy is recommended.

items.foco: Stage IIIA: invasion of serosa or adnexa
items.title: Stage IIIA (FIGO)
items.contentHtml:
Stage IIIA: invasion of serosa or adnexa

  • The stage IIIA according to the International Federation of Gynecology and Obstetrics (FIGO) 2009 classification is defined by tumor invasion of the uterine serosa and/or involvement of adnexa (ovary, tube) by direct extension or metastasis.
  • It is subclassified: IIIA1 (invasion of serosa) and IIIA2 (annexal commitment); both involve locally advanced disease and require adjuvant treatment.
  • Management includes total hysterectomy with bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy, lymphadenectomy, and adjuvant platinum- and taxane-based chemotherapy (carboplatin + paclitaxel).
  • Pelvic radiotherapy may be added depending on risk factors; 5-year survival is 50-70%.

items.foco: Recurrence: vaginal metastasis is the most common
items.title: Vaginal recurrence and metastasis
items.contentHtml:
Recurrence: vaginal metastasis is the most common

  • Recurrence of endometrial cancer occurs in 10-20% of cases; Vaginal metastasis (vaginal dome) is the most common location of local relapse (50-70% of isolated recurrences).
  • It presents as a nodule or bleeding in the vaginal dome; the diagnosis is confirmed by biopsy.
  • Treatment of isolated vaginal recurrence includes radiotherapy (vaginal brachytherapy ± external radiotherapy) with local control rates of 80-90%.
  • Distant recurrences (lung, liver, peritoneum) are more common in Type II and require systemic chemotherapy; The prognosis is guarded (survival <2 years).
